1: The oracle is undated — the only undated oracle in the Egypt collection, suggesting it may have been composed as a generalized theological statement about the Day of the LORD rather than a specific dated prophecy. The undated status places it in contrast with the precisely dated oracles surrounding it (Ezekiel 29:1, Ezekiel 29:17, Ezekiel 30:20, Ezekiel 31:1, Ezekiel 32:1, Ezekiel 32:17), highlighting that the “Day of the LORD is near” is not a calculation but a theological certainty.
2: “Wail — alas for the day!” (heyliyl ha yom) — the prophetic wail (heyliyl, howl) is the sound of professional mourners at a death. Amos 8:3 uses it for the day when temple songs become wailing; Isaiah 13:6 uses it for the Day of the LORD against Babylon; Isaiah 23:1 for the oracle against Tyre. Ezekiel applies it here to Egypt. The “day” language anticipates Ezekiel 30:3's explicit “Day of the LORD” — the wailing is appropriate because the Day of the LORD, though salvation for Israel in some contexts, is purely judgment in its application to the nations who rebelled against YHWH's cosmic order.
3: “The day of the LORD is near — a day of cloud. It will be the time of the nations” (ki qarov yom vaqarov yom-YHWH anan et-goyim yihyeh) — the Yom YHWH tradition in the prophets originated as a day when YHWH would intervene decisively in history on behalf of Israel (originally assumed to be automatically favorable: Amos 5:18-20 corrects this assumption for Israel herself). In the foreign-nation oracles it is unambiguously judgment. “A day of cloud” (anan) recalls theophanic cloud-presence (the pillar of cloud in the wilderness, the cloud filling the tabernacle and temple) — but here it is a war-cloud: dark, threatening, obscuring. “Time of the nations” (et goyim) applies the Day of the LORD to all nations simultaneously, not just Egypt.
4: “Anguish will grip Ethiopia when the slain fall in Egypt; her wealth will be carried off” — Cush (Ethiopia/Nubia) was not merely Egypt's neighbor but had recently been Egypt's rulers: the 25th Dynasty (ca. 744-656 BCE) was Nubian, and Cushite kings like Taharqa and Tantamani had ruled Egypt and fought Assyria. By Ezekiel's time, Cush was a separate kingdom again (the 26th Dynasty was Saite/Egyptian), but deep cultural and economic ties remained. Cushite mercenaries served in the Egyptian army (Jeremiah 46:9; Nahum 3:9). Egypt's fall will destabilize Cush economically and militarily.
5: The coalition of Egypt's allies — Cush (Nubia), Put (Libya), Lud (possibly Lydia — Lydian mercenaries in the Egyptian army are mentioned by Herodotus), Cub (uncertain; possibly a Libyan tribe or Galatian mercenaries), and the “mixed peoples” (erev, literally “mixture/mingling”, referring to foreign mercenaries and residents in Egypt) — reflects the cosmopolitan military character of the Egyptian army in the Saite period. Herodotus (2.152, 154) describes the Saite kings bringing in Ionian and Carian mercenaries; the Egyptian military in this period was notable for its reliance on foreign troops. The “people of the land that is in league with them” (uvney eretz habberit) may refer to Judean refugees in Egypt (Jer 44) or to treaty-allied Canaanite states.
6: “Those who support Egypt will fall” — the entire security system of Egyptian alliances collapses together with Egypt itself. The covenant-partner nations that relied on Egypt (as Judah had done) will discover that Egypt cannot save them either. “From Migdol to Syene they will fall by the sword within her” — the polar-extremes formula from 29:10 is repeated, confirming the totality of the judgment. The word “proud strength” (geion uzah) echoes the same phrase used of Israel's pride in Leviticus 26:19 (“I will break down your proud strength”) — Egypt receives the same covenant-curse language that Israel received.
7: “Desolate in the midst of desolated lands, her cities will lie among ruined cities” — the description situates Egypt within the broader landscape of Babylonian conquest in the 6th century. Judah, Moab, Ammon, Philistia, Tyre, and Sidon were all in various states of destruction or subjugation. Egypt in ruins is not uniquely targeted by YHWH but joins the community of ruined nations — the comprehensive collapse of the pre-exilic political order. The Babylonian period was one of the great demographic and political disruptions of the ancient Near East, and Ezekiel's oracles interpret this disruption as YHWH's comprehensive judgments against pride and covenant-breach.
8: “They will know that I am the LORD when I set fire to Egypt and all her helpers are broken” — the knowledge formula applied to Egypt's destruction follows the same theological logic as in the Israel oracles: YHWH's actions produce knowledge of his identity. “Set fire to Egypt” is likely the military vocabulary of torch-and-siege (ancient armies routinely burned cities after conquest). The Babylonian destruction of Egyptian cities is attested in fragmentary records of Nebuchadnezzar's 568-567 BCE campaign (the Berlin fragments of the Babylonian Chronicle mention his Egyptian campaign). The “breaking of Egypt's helpers” means the end of the coalition described in Ezekiel 30:4-6.
9: “Messengers will go out from me by ship to terrify the complacent Cush” — the news of Egypt's fall will travel by sea to Nubia/Ethiopia. The Nile-to-Red-Sea river system was a major commercial and communication route in the ancient world; Egyptian ships regularly traveled these waters. “Complacent Cush” (Kush habetoah) — confident in its remoteness and security — will be shaken when it learns that Egypt, its enormous northern neighbor and former master, has fallen. The same “complacent” language is used of Babylon in Isaiah 47:8 (“you who feel secure, who say in your heart I am and there is no one else besides me”).
10: “I will put an end to the multitude of Egypt by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon” — Nebuchadnezzar is named explicitly as YHWH's instrument against Egypt. This naming of a foreign king as YHWH's servant echoes Isaiah 44:28-45:1 (Cyrus named as shepherd and anointed) and Jeremiah 25:9 (Nebuchadnezzar named as YHWH's servant against the nations). The “multitude of Egypt” (hamon Mitzrayim) is the vast military and population mass of Egypt — the single most powerful empire in the region at this time. That YHWH would use Babylon against Egypt is the ultimate statement of Babylonian hegemony as divinely appointed.
11: “The most ruthless of nations” (arizey goyim) is Ezekiel's characteristic epithet for the Babylonian army (also in Ezekiel 28:7; Ezekiel 31:12; Ezekiel 32:12). The Babylonian army's reputation for ruthlessness was historically well-founded: the destruction of Jerusalem (Lamentations 1:12-15; Ezekiel 2:1-9), the deportations, and the systematic dismantlement of subject kingdoms were all documented. The phrase functions as a paradoxical theological pointer: the most brutal human army is the instrument of divine justice. “Unsheathing swords against Egypt” — the sword-drawing gesture is the formal commencement of combat in ancient military protocol.
12: “I will make the Nile channels run dry” (vehoravti yeorot venatarti aretz beyad reim) — the Nile's seven branches (the Delta channels) were the geographic foundation of Lower Egypt's civilization. Blocking or diverting Nile channels was a known military tactic in the ancient world. The verb “sell the land into the hand of evil men” (venatarti aretz beyad reim) is commercial: YHWH is the divine landlord who transfers Egypt's lease to Babylon. “By the hand of foreigners” (beyad zarim) — the same phrase used in Ezekiel 28:7 for Tyre's destruction by strangers; YHWH delivers his owned territory to tenants of his choice.
13: “I will destroy the idols and put an end to the images in Memphis” — Memphis (Hebrew Nof or Mof, Egyptian Men-nefer) was the ancient administrative capital of Lower Egypt and the center of Ptah-worship. Ptah was the creator-deity of Memphis, the divine craftsman who created by speaking the word in his heart and on his tongue — a remarkable structural parallel to the Logos theology of Gen 1 and John 1. The “images” (elilim) of Memphis included Ptah, Sekhmet (lion-headed war-goddess), and Apis (the sacred bull). “No longer a prince in the land of Egypt” (velo-yihyeh od nasi meeretz mitzrayim) — after Alexander's conquest (332 BCE) and then Roman annexation (30 BCE), native Egyptian rule effectively ended.
14: Pathros (Upper Egypt), Zoan (Tanis), and Thebes (No) are three major centers of Egyptian political and religious power. Zoan/Tanis (Tso-an) in the Delta was the northern capital during the Tanite dynasties and was a major political center in the Saite period; the psalm tradition remembered Egypt by reference to Zoan (Psalms 78:12, 43). Thebes (Egyptian Waset, Hebrew No or No-Amon) was the great southern capital and the center of Amun worship — its temples at Karnak and Luxor were the largest religious complex in the ancient world. The fall of Thebes to the Assyrians under Ashurbanipal in 663 BCE (referenced in Nahum 3:8-10) was a living memory in Ezekiel's time — Egypt had rebuilt, but the memory of that earlier destruction provided a precedent for what Ezekiel now announces.
15: Pelusium (Hebrew Sin, Egyptian Per-Amun) was the fortified northeastern gateway to Egypt — the key military position controlling the land route from Asia into Egypt. Every major invasion of Egypt from the east (Assyrian, Babylonian, Persian, Macedonian) passed through or near Pelusium. Cambyses defeated Egypt at Pelusium in 525 BCE; Alexander encountered it in 332 BCE. The “stronghold of Egypt” (mivtzar Mitzrayim) title is apt: Pelusium was Egypt's first line of defense and its most fortified city. Thebes appears again — the double mention of Thebes (Ezekiel 30:14 and 15-16) reflects its importance; it was Egypt's theological and cultural heart.
16: The city-catalog pattern in Ezekiel 30:13-19 is similar to the Egyptian Execration Texts (clay figurines inscribed with enemies' names and ritually broken) and to the list of cities cursed in Amos 1-2 — the naming of cities in a judgment oracle both specifics and makes the judgment concretely applicable. “Pelusium will writhe in anguish, Thebes will be breached, Memphis will face daily distress” — three different verbs of suffering (hil/writhe, hivvaqea/breach, tsarot yomam/daily distress) applied to three of Egypt's greatest cities gives the oracle a varied rhetoric that prevents it from being merely formulaic repetition.
17: On (Greek Heliopolis, Egyptian Iunu) was the ancient seat of Re/Atum worship and the center of Egyptian solar theology. The great obelisks of Heliopolis (two of which are now the “Cleopatra's Needles” in London and New York) were sacred to the sun-god. The biblical connection: Joseph's father-in-law Potiphera was a priest at On (Genesis 41:45), and Moses likely had some exposure to its religious tradition. Bubastis (Pi-beseth, Egyptian Per-Bastet) was the center of Bastet worship — the cat/lion-headed goddess — and served as a capital during the 22nd-23rd Dynasties. Herodotus (2.59-60) describes its famous annual festival with great crowds traveling by ship on the Nile canals.
18: Tahpanhes (Hebrew Tachpanes, Greek Daphnai, modern Tell Defenneh) was a Delta fortress city near the northeastern border where the Egyptian army maintained a garrison. After Jerusalem's fall (586 BCE), Johanan son of Kareah led Judean refugees to Tahpanhes against Jeremiah's counsel (Jeremiah 43:1-7), and Jeremiah himself ended his ministry there. The clay bricks he buried at Tahpanhes (Jeremiah 43:9-10) were the sign of Nebuchadnezzar's throne being set there. Archaeological excavations at Tell Defenneh revealed a large brick platform and Egyptian garrison fortress, confirming the biblical description. The “breaking of Egypt's yokes” (shavar matotvot Mitzrayim) = the end of Egyptian imperial power over subject nations.
19: “Thus I will execute judgments in Egypt, and they will know that I am the LORD” — the closing knowledge formula seals the city-catalog oracle. The executed judgments are not arbitrary violence but legal sentences: YHWH is the divine judge executing covenant-curses against a nation that violated the created moral order (claiming divine status, making the Nile its own, functioning as a broken staff to dependent peoples). The plural “judgments” (shfatim) echoes the Exodus vocabulary — in Exodus 7:4 YHWH brings “great acts of judgment” against Egypt through the plagues; here the same word describes the Babylonian conquest as a second round of divine judgment on Egypt.
20: The date — “eleventh year, first month, seventh day” = April 29, 587 BCE — is one of only three oracles dated to the active siege period (alongside 24:1 and Ezekiel 29:1). The siege of Jerusalem is ongoing; the context is Hophra's recent failed relief attempt. This oracle provides the theological explanation for the failed intervention: YHWH has broken Pharaoh's arm and will not heal it. The date in the first month (spring) suggests proximity to the Passover — YHWH's original act of judgment against Egypt — making the broken-arm oracle a commentary on the theological continuity: what YHWH did to Egypt at the Exodus, he is doing again.
21: “I have broken the arm of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and it has not been bound up to heal it” — the “arm” (zeroa) is the standard symbol of military power in the OT (YHWH's “mighty arm” in the Exodus; the warrior's bared arm in Isaiah 52:10; Ezekiel 4:7). Pharaoh Hophra (Greek Apries, reigned 589-570 BCE) had marched his army toward Jerusalem (Jeremiah 37:5-11) — the Babylonians temporarily lifted the siege, Judean defenders relaxed, and then Hophra withdrew without engaging, the Babylonians returned, and Jerusalem's fate was sealed. “Not bound up to heal it” — YHWH refuses to allow Egypt's military recovery; the arm broken by Hophra's failure will remain broken. The healing-of-wounds imagery echoes Hosea 5:13 (Israel seeking healing from Assyria and finding none) and Jeremiah 30:12-13 (YHWH refusing to bind up Israel's wounds in judgment).
22: “I will break his arms, both the strong one and the already broken one” — the “strong arm” is the remaining military capacity after Hophra's failed expedition; the “already broken arm” is the withdrawal from Jerusalem. YHWH will complete the disable: Egypt's future military campaigns will also fail. The historical correspondence: Hophra was eventually overthrown by his own general Ahmose II (Amasis, ca. 570 BCE) after a military disaster in Libya — the final confirmation that Egypt's arms were broken. “I will make the sword fall from his hand” — the dramatic image of a warrior's sword dropping from a broken arm; Egypt will not be able to wield it.
23: “I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations” — the scattering of Egypt among the nations (Ezekiel 30:23) and the strengthening of Babylon's arms (Ezekiel 30:24) are parallel and opposite actions: YHWH weakens the one and strengthens the other. The scattering of a defeated people across the known world was standard Babylonian administrative policy — attested in the deportations of Israelites, Judeans, Phoenicians, and eventually Egyptians. Ezekiel's exilic community would have recognized this vocabulary immediately as the experience they were living.
24: “I will strengthen the arms of the king of Babylon and put my sword in his hand; but I will break Pharaoh's arms” — the spatial opposition (Babylon's arms strengthened; Pharaoh's broken) makes the transfer of divine mandate explicit. The sword that falls from Pharaoh's broken arm (Ezekiel 30:22) is placed in Nebuchadnezzar's hand (Ezekiel 30:24). This is the theological heart of Ezekiel 29-32: the transfer of world-historical primacy from Egypt to Babylon is YHWH's sovereign act, not merely the outcome of military superiority. “He will groan before him with the groaning of a mortally wounded man” (venehom lefanaiv nehamat chalal) — the death-moan of a war-casualty is the metaphor for Egypt's defeat-cry before Nebuchadnezzar.
25: The knowledge formula appears again (“they will know that I am the LORD”) but with additional specification: “when I put my sword in the hand of the king of Babylon and he extends it over the land of Egypt.” The knowing that YHWH is LORD comes through Nebuchadnezzar's sword. This is consistent with Ezekiel's consistent use of Babylonian military victory as YHWH's self-revelation instrument — even the instruments of judgment serve the divine purpose of making YHWH known. The nations, including Egypt, will come to know YHWH not through blessing and prosperity but through the experience of his sovereignty in judgment.
26: The oracle concludes with the scattering-and-knowing formula one final time: “I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations and disperse them through the lands; and they will know that I am the LORD.” The pairing of scattering with knowing has a paradoxical quality: Egypt scattered among the nations will know YHWH — but only in the context of displacement and defeat. The Egyptian diaspora created by Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns and the later Persian conquest would have brought Egyptians into contact with Israelite and Jewish communities who could explain the theological meaning of these events. Knowledge of YHWH comes through suffering, not through success — a consistent theme in Ezekiel's foreign-nation oracles.
